likely_position: Counsel would most likely defend the surcharge as a valid property-related fee (not a tax), leaning heavily on the 1.6× marginal-cost finding to argue the tier differential is substantially cost-justified and characterizing "conservation-incentive pricing" as merely reflecting real peaking/drought-reserve costs rather than a freestanding policy add-on — distinguishing Capistrano on the ground that some cost nexus exists here (unlike the purely incentive-based tiers struck down there). As a pleaded fallback, counsel would argue the turf-rebate/recycled-water/education spending serves system-wide reliability benefiting all ratepayers (including surcharge payers) even though rebate checks go to others, and would resist the "it's a tax" characterization since a fee loses only the excess-over-cost portion in refunds while a tax could require full Prop 26 election and voided revenue. In parallel, expect counsel to quietly recommend a defensive rate restructuring or settlement — trimming the surcharge toward the defensible 1.6× multiple and moving the rebate/education components to a non-fee funding source — to cap refund exposure (Gov. Code § 53750 et seq.) and potential fee-shifting (Code Civ. Proc. § 1021.5) rather than litigate the full $0.85 figure to judgment.
